did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a deep learning-based system for automatic melanoma detection and segmentation. It utilizes an enhanced encoder-decoder network (Deep Convolutional Network) with skip pathways, a softmax classifier for pixel-wise classification, and a dice loss function. The method was evaluated on the ISIC 2017 and PH2 skin lesion datasets, achieving 95% accuracy and 92-93% dice coefficient.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel deep learning-based system (enhanced encoder-decoder network) for automatic melanoma detection and segmentation. It details specific methods, including a Lesion-classifier and dice loss function, and presents original experimental results on the ISIC 2017 and PH2 datasets, achieving 95% accuracy and 92-93% dice coefficient. The structure includes Introduction, Related Works, Methodology, Experiments, and Results, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Deep Learning-Based System for Automatic Melanoma Detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning-based system for automatic melanoma detection and segmentation to address the challenges of complex visual characteristics in skin lesion images. The method utilizes an enhanced encoder-decoder network with skip pathways, a softmax classifier for pixel-wise classification, and a novel Lesion-classifier to distinguish melanoma from non-melanoma lesions. The system was evaluated on two public benchmark datasets: the ISIC 2017 dataset and the PH2 dataset. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 95% and a dice coefficient of 92% on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and 95% accuracy with a 93% dice coefficient on the PH2 dataset. These results indicate that the proposed approach is more effective than several state-of-the-art methods in terms of recognition and localization performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for detection and segmentation. The paper focuses on distinguishing melanoma lesions from non-melanoma lesions using the ISIC 2017 and PH2 datasets.
The negative class used for binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). The paper groups all non-melanoma lesions into this single category for the Lesion-classifier task.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise classification of melanoma lesions (binary mask)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an enhanced encoder-decoder network for automatic melanoma lesion detection and segmentation. It utilizes a softmax classifier for pixel-wise classification and a dice loss function. The system produces segmentation masks for melanoma lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper devises a 'Lesion-classifier' that performs binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma based on the results derived from pixel-wise classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopy dataset extracted from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, provided by the ISIC Dermoscopic Archive for the 2017 challenge. It contains dermoscopy images with ground truth labels provided by experts.

use.training: Used for supervised training of the proposed deep learning-based system (enhanced encoder-decoder network).
use.validation: Ground truth labels provided for evaluating validation phase data.
use.testing: Used to test the model, achieving 95% accuracy and 92% dice coefficient.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopy dataset obtained from the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano. It contains skin lesion images in JPEG format with corresponding ground truth mask images in PNG format.

use.training: Used for training the proposed model (specifically noted as requiring 250 training epochs due to dataset size).
use.testing: Used to test the model on 50 images, achieving 95% accuracy and 93% dice coefficient.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and dice coefficients compared against state-of-the-art methods without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.notes: The proposed model was trained on 2000 training images over 35 epochs. Training time was approximately 350s per epoch, and test time per single image was around 5s. The model outperformed state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and dice score.

PH2.notes: The model was tested on 50 images. It required 250 training epochs due to the smaller dataset size (200 images total). The results indicate the system can identify and differentiate affected skin lesions from healthy tissues effectively.

architecture_details.encoder_structure: Five blocks; each block contains two 3x3 convolutional layers, one max pooling layer, and a ReLU non-linear activation function
architecture_details.decoder_structure: Five blocks; upsampling using nearest neighbor with 2x2 convolutional layers, concatenated with corresponding encoder feature maps via skip pathways, followed by convolutional layers and ReLU activation
architecture_details.upsampling: Nearest neighbor upsampling with 2x2 convolutional layers
architecture_details.skip_connections: Series of skip pathways connecting encoder and decoder sub-networks to bring semantic levels closer

training_details.loss_function: Dice loss function combined with softmax classifier
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7 processor (10 cores, 3.4 GHz), NVIDIA Tesla K40c GPU, 148.5 TB shared memory
training_details.software: Python 3.5.2, Keras 2, TensorFlow backend 1.1.0, TFlearn 0.3, Scikit-image 0.14.1
training_details.data_augmentation: Random displacement, simple and random rotation, pixel intensity remapping to range [-0.5, 0.5]

classification_or_output_head.post_processing: Lesion-classifier module that clusters predicted pixels to distinguish melanoma from non-melanoma

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filtering for noise and artifact removal (air, hair, bubbles)
Cropping, resizing, and re-sampling to standardize scale and resolution
Data centring normalization using mean pixel value and standard deviation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Utilized local distortion and random affine transformation with random displacement to improve output quality.
Applied as part of the elastic deformation process with random displacement.
Adopted in the augmentation process to improve performance.
Linear remapping of intensities to the range [-0.5, 0.5] to center pixel intensity around zero and provide numerical stability during training.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning-based system, utilizing an enhanced encoder-decoder network with skip pathways and a Lesion-classifier, effectively addresses the challenges of complex visual characteristics in skin lesion images, such as fuzzy boundaries and inhomogeneous features.
The method achieved an accuracy of 95% and a dice coefficient of 92% on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and an accuracy of 95% and a dice coefficient of 93% on the PH2 dataset.
The proposed model outperformed several state-of-the-art methods, including FrCN, U-NET, and FCN, in terms of accuracy, dice score, sensitivity, and specificity on both evaluated datasets.

The system is capable of accurately identifying and differentiating melanoma lesions from non-melanoma lesions and healthy tissues, demonstrating its potential as an effective Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) tool.
The use of a dice loss function combined with a softmax classifier contributed to the model's ability to reach high accuracy percentages, particularly on the larger ISIC 2017 dataset.

The study acknowledges that segmentation performance can improve with increased training steps and larger dataset sizes, as evidenced by the need for 250 epochs on the smaller PH2 dataset compared to 35 epochs on the larger ISIC 2017 dataset.
important_remarks: The paper was published in IEEE Access, which is a predatory or low-quality journal; the content may not have undergone rigorous peer review.
The study relies on two public datasets (ISIC 2017 and PH2) without external validation on independent clinical data, limiting generalizability.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images total), requiring 250 training epochs, which may lead to overfitting or biased results.
The ISIC 2017 dataset has a large size (2000 training images), which may contribute to inflated performance metrics compared to smaller datasets.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with state-of-the-art methods, making it unclear if performance differences are significant.

The model's performance on the PH2 dataset (95% accuracy, 93% dice coefficient) is comparable to the ISIC 2017 dataset, which is unusual given the significant difference in dataset size and complexity.
The paper does not discuss potential data leakage or preprocessing biases that could affect the results.
The study focuses on melanoma detection and segmentation but does not address the clinical relevance or deployment challenges in real-world dermatology practice.
